items.q: How do you write 'Christmas' and 'Easter' in Spanish?
items.correct: Navidad y Pascua (note: Semana Santa for Holy Week traditions).
items.why: Students add unnecessary accents or use English. Navidad has NO accent (stress on the final syllable but ends in -d which is naturally stressed). Pascua follows normal stress rules. Customs vocab: Reyes Magos (Three Kings), feria (fair).

items.q: What is the 'nosotros' form of 'hablar' (to speak)?
items.why: Students confuse nosotros (we) with ellos (they). AR verbs in present: hablo, hablas, habla, HABLAMOS, habláis, hablan. The nosotros ending is -amos, not -an.

items.q: Why does 'Me gusta el chocolate' literally translate as 'Chocolate is pleasing to me'?
items.correct: Gustar works backwards - the thing liked is the subject, the person is the indirect object.
items.misconception: Gustar means 'to like' and works exactly like English.
items.random_wrong: It is a typo for 'Yo gusto'.
items.why: Students translate gustar as 'to like' with English structure. Actually gustar = 'to be pleasing to'. So 'me gustan los libros' (books are pleasing to me) - the verb agrees with the thing, not the liker.

items.q: Which adjective never changes for gender?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Grande. “Rojo” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Roja. “Rojos” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many letters do you change to make rojo into roja?
items.why: The real figure is 1. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
